title: Future Tense
situation: Make plans, promises, and predictions, talk about what's going to happen

phrases.english: I will do (male) / I will do (female)
phrases.context: Future: verb root + unga/ungi (for 'main'). Male = -unga, female = -ungi. Simple!

phrases.english: You will study (intimate-male) / You will study (intimate-female)
phrases.context: With 'tu': verb root + ega/egi. 'tu' is VERY informal, close-friends/playful only, sounds rude otherwise. Default to 'aap' or 'tum'. The ga/gi gender split applies throughout.

phrases.english: You will do (casual-male) / You will do (casual-female)
phrases.context: With 'tum': verb root + oge/ogi. The 'o' sound is the 'tum' marker in future

phrases.english: He will do / She will do
phrases.context: 'woh' uses same form as 'tu': -ega/-egi. Third person and intimate-you share the form

phrases.english: We will do (male group) / We will do (female group)
phrases.context: 'hum' = -enge/-engi. Also used for 'aap' (formal you) and 'woh' (they). The respectful/plural form

phrases.hindi: main kal jaaunga / main kal jaaungi
phrases.english: I'll go tomorrow (male) / I'll go tomorrow (female)
phrases.pronunciation: MAIN KAL jaa-UN-ga / MAIN KAL jaa-UN-gi

phrases.context: 'kab' = when. Just add question word, verb form doesn't change. Rising intonation optional

phrases.english: I'm coming right now (I'll be right there)
phrases.context: TRICK: Present tense used for immediate future! 'abhi aata hoon' = coming right now. Very common
'hogi' = will be/will happen (feminine, because 'baarish' is feminine). 'hona' / hoga/hogi/honge
Non-committal future! 'dekhte hain' = let's see. Indians use this to avoid saying no directly

grammar_notes: Future tense endings by person: main = -unga/-ungi, tu/woh = -ega/-egi, tum = -oge/-ogi, hum/aap/woh(plural) = -enge/-engi
Pattern: take verb root + the suffix. 'jaana' / root 'jaa': jaaunga, jaayega, jaaoge, jaayenge. 'khaana' / root 'khaa': khaaunga, khaayega, khaaoge, khaayenge
The gender split is always -ga (male) vs -gi (female) at the end. This is the one consistent rule across all persons
'hona' (to be) in future: hoga (he/it-masc will be), hogi (she/it-fem will be), honge (they/we will be), houngi (I-fem will be)
Shortcut: Present tense + 'abhi/ab' often replaces future for immediate plans. 'Main abhi aata hoon' (I'm coming now) instead of 'main aaunga'
culture_notes: 'Dekhte hain' (let's see) is the polite Indian no. If someone says this about your plan, it probably won't happen
Indians frequently use 'kal' without specifying yesterday vs tomorrow, you're expected to figure it out. If confused, ask 'beeta kal ya aane wala kal?'

skill_breakdown.explanation: First person feminine: '-ungi' (main jaaungi). Masculine: '-unga'. Second/third: '-egi'/'-ega', plural: '-engi'/'-enge'. Pattern: stem + gender-marker + person-marker.
'-na hai' = soft obligation (I have to / I need to). '-na padega' = stronger obligation (I'll have to / will be forced to). Subject takes 'ko' marker: 'mujhe', 'aapko'.

more_examples.hindi: mujhe doctor ke paas jaana padega
more_examples.english: I'll have to go to the doctor

more_examples.hindi: hum sabko milkar kaam karna padega
more_examples.english: we'll all have to work together

skill_breakdown.explanation: 'pakka' (definitely / for sure) and 'zaroor' (certainly) are essential filler-words that make a future promise sound sincere. Drop them and you sound noncommittal.

practice_prompt: You are a Hindi tutor drilling future tense, the `-ga/-gi/-ge` endings, gender agreement, and `agar...toh` conditionals. Use `aap` throughout. Open with: `chaliye future tense practice karte hain, bataaiye Hindi mein: 'I will come tomorrow', aapka jawab kya hoga?` Then drill one task at a time: translate an English sentence, conjugate a given verb for a specific pronoun, transform a present-tense sentence into future, or build a conditional `agar...toh` sentence. Mix 6-8 items of increasing difficulty and include at least one `agar...toh` conditional and one polite soft-future request (e.g. `aap chai piyenge?`). Focus on `-ga` (m sg) / `-gi` (f sg or f pl) / `-ge` (m pl or aap) endings, subject-gender agreement, conditional structures, and polite future framing. When the user slips, flag it with `[[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]]`, watch especially for wrong gender on the ending (the most common error), `agar` clauses missing `toh`, and English-style `will + verb` ordering. Don't switch to English unless asked. End with `aapne kaafi achi practice ki, phir milte hain`.

theory.intro: Hindi builds the future tense into the verb itself. There's no separate `will` word. You take the verb stem and add `-ga` (masculine), `-gi` (feminine), or `-ge` (plural or respect). The trick is making the ending agree with the subject's gender, which English speakers consistently miss at first. Once the endings click, the future is the most regular of the three tenses you've learned.

sections.heading: The simple future shape
sections.body:
Hindi future = **verb stem** + a person-and-gender ending (suffix). There's no separate `will` word. The stem is what's left after you strip `-na` from the infinitive: - `karna` (to do) / stem `kar`
- `aana` (to come) / stem `aa`
- `khaana` (to eat) / stem `khaa` The ending changes by both **person** and **gender**. Every future sentence uses one of the six endings in the table below.
sections.cutting_intro: Hi! The future is the easiest of the three tenses. No `will` word, just stem plus ending. Let's see the shape.

quick_check.question: What ending does a female speaker use for `main` (I) in the future tense?
quick_check.explanation: Right, `-ungi` for 1st-person feminine. A male speaker would say `karunga`; a female speaker says `karungi`. The `-ng-` is the 1st-person marker, the final `-a` vs `-i` is the gender flip.

sections.heading: Gender + number agreement
sections.body:
Like the present and past, the future ending agrees with the **subject's gender**. The verb alone tells the listener whether the speaker is male or female. Pick the ending from these three shapes: - masculine singular / `-ga` (`karunga`, `karega`)
- feminine / `-gi` (`karungi`, `karegi`, `karengi`)
- masculine plural / `-ge` (`karenge`, `karoge`) `Aap` (formal you) **always** takes the plural `-enge`/`-engi` form, even for one person. It's the same politeness-as-plurality pattern that runs through all of Hindi.

examples.english: When will you come? (formal, addressing one person respectfully)
examples.breakdown: `aap` always takes plural `-enge`, even for one person

callout.body: Feminine plural still uses `-gi`, not `-gain` or any pluralized variant. So `ladki jaayegi` (one girl will go) and `ladkiyaan jaayengi` (girls will go) both end in `-gi`. It's a historical irregularity, feminine never pluralizes the future ending itself. Just trust it.

sections.cutting_intro: Now the ending has to match the subject's gender. Get it right and you sound natural. Get it wrong and locals will gently correct you.

quick_check.question: Which future form does Hindi use with `aap` (formal you)?
quick_check.explanation: Always plural. `Aap` takes `-enge`/`-engi` even when addressing one person, politeness uses the plural form. Same pattern across present and past tense too.

sections.heading: Polite requests + suggestions
sections.body:
A future-tense question is Hindi's everyday **polite request**. `Aap kal aayenge?` (will you come tomorrow?) is much softer than the command `aaiye` (come). Framing it as a question gives the person room to decline gracefully.

The same trick works for offers. `Aap chai piyenge?` (will you drink chai?) is how a host offers tea. You'd never use the imperative `chai piyiye`. The future question hands the guest some agency.

Note: `hum chalein?` (shall we go?) uses the subjunctive, a separate form, but in conversation it works just like a soft future suggestion.

table.caption: Imperative vs future-as-request
rows: tell me / will you tell me?

callout.body: When in doubt, frame it as a future question. `Aap chai piyenge?` (will you drink chai?) is the warm, hospitable Indian offer, not the imperative `chai piyiye`. The future form gives the listener agency.

sections.cutting_intro: Here's a real-world trick. The future tense is also Hindi's polite-request register. You'll hear it everywhere.

quick_check.question: Which form sounds more polite when offering chai?
quick_check.explanation: Future-question is softer. `Aap chai piyenge?` invites; `chai piyiye!` instructs. Hosts use the future framing because it gives the guest room to decline.

sections.heading: The conditional: agar...to...
sections.body:
The most common real-world use of the future tense is the **conditional** sentence. `Agar` introduces the condition (if). `to` introduces the result (then).

Here's the key difference from English: in Hindi, **both** clauses use future tense, even where English uses the present in the 'if' clause. So `if it rains, we won't go` becomes `agar baarish hogi, to hum nahin jaayenge`. `hogi` is future, not present.

examples.hindi: agar baarish hogi, to hum nahin jaayenge
examples.english: if it rains, we won't go

examples.hindi: agar tum aaoge, to main bhi aaunga
examples.english: if you come, then I'll come too

examples.hindi: agar samay hoga, to phone karungi
examples.english: if I have time, I'll call (fem)
examples.breakdown: `samay hoga` = time will be (= I'll have time)

callout.body: In casual speech, `to` is often dropped. `Agar tum aaoge, main bhi aaunga` is perfectly natural, the comma carries the `then`. Listen for this; native speakers skip `to` constantly in fast speech.

sections.cutting_intro: Here's the most useful pattern of all: `agar...to...`, if...then. Practice this and you've got half of conversational future covered.

quick_check.question: In `agar baarish hogi, to hum nahin jaayenge`, what tense is `hogi`?
quick_check.explanation: Future. Hindi puts BOTH clauses of `agar...to...` in future tense, even though English would say `if it rains` (present). `Hogi` = will be / will happen.

sections.heading: Common pitfalls
sections.body:
**Trap 1: the phantom `will`.** English speakers try `main will khaata`. But Hindi has no `will` word. The future meaning lives inside the ending. `I will eat` = `main khaaunga`, one word doing all the work. **Trap 2: wrong ending for the pronoun.** Each pronoun locks in a specific ending shape. The wrong one makes the sentence ungrammatical, not just awkward. - `main` / always `-unga` / `-ungi`
- `tu` / `woh` / always `-ega` / `-egi`
- `tum` / always `-oge` / `-ogi`
- `hum` / `aap` / `woh` (plural) / always `-enge` / `-engi` The examples below show the correct form next to the common mistake.

callout.body: Pronoun and ending must match. `Main` / `-unga`/`-ungi`. `Tu`/`woh` / `-ega`/`-egi`. `Tum` / `-oge`/`-ogi`. `Hum`/`aap`/`woh (pl)` / `-enge`/`-engi`. Mix these up and the sentence becomes ungrammatical, not just awkward, but wrong. Drill the pronoun-ending pairs until they're automatic.

sections.cutting_intro: Heads up: these are the slips I see learners make every day. Watch out for them.

quick_check.question: What's wrong with `main khaayega` (intended: I will eat)?
quick_check.explanation: Right, `-ega` is for `tu`/`woh`, not `main`. The correct form is `main khaaunga` (masc) or `main khaaungi` (fem). Pronoun and ending have to match.

theory.wrap_up: The future is mechanical once you internalize the endings: verb stem plus the right `-ga`/`-gi`/`-ge` for the subject. Drill the endings until they're automatic, then practice `agar...to` conditionals for the most common real-world use. The phrases below cover plans, predictions, and polite requests. Notice which pronoun pairs with which ending as you read them.
